questions.prompt: When you snap, go cold, over-explain, or agree to something you already know you will resent, when do you usually see what is happening?

options.label: I rarely see it at all.
I usually see it much later.
I see it once I have calmed down.
I often see it while it is happening.
I can usually see it and choose differently.

questions.prompt: When you look at your ambition, standards, relationship patterns, and daily life, how often can you tell what is genuinely yours and what you inherited?

options.label: I rarely question it. I mostly live what was expected.
I can see some of it, usually after the fact.
I can tell the difference in some areas.
I can usually feel what is mine and what is inherited.
I can see the inheritance clearly and choose from what is genuinely mine.

questions.prompt: When the same difficulty keeps returning, where does your attention usually go first?

options.label: To what other people are doing wrong.
Mostly to blame, frustration, or defence.
Some blame, some self-inquiry.
To what is mine to see or change.
To what is mine, what is theirs, and what reality is showing me.

questions.prompt: When there is no role to fill, no one to impress, and nothing to achieve, how clear are you about who is actually there?

options.label: Not clear. It feels uncomfortable.
I get glimpses, but I avoid staying there.
Somewhat clear.
Mostly clear.
Clear enough that I do not need performance to feel real.

questions.prompt: When someone says something that stings, how much space is there between what you feel and what you do next?

options.label: Almost none. I react immediately.
Very little. I usually realise after.
Some. I catch myself occasionally.
Usually enough to pause.
Enough to feel it, stay present, and choose my response.

questions.prompt: Before a difficult meeting, avoided conversation, or decision that feels wrong, how often do you notice what your body already knows?

options.label: Rarely. I push straight through.
Occasionally.
Sometimes.
Often.
Consistently. My body is part of how I read reality.

questions.prompt: When you sense something unspoken in someone close to you, what do you usually do?

options.label: I miss it or ignore it.
I sense something, but avoid naming it.
I assume I know what is happening.
I stay curious and check rather than assume.
I can sense, check cleanly, and let them have their reality.

questions.prompt: In a high-stakes moment, how much of you is genuinely present rather than managing how you are coming across?

options.label: Almost all of me is managing the impression.
More managing than being present.
About half and half.
Mostly present.
I can stay real even when I am being watched or challenged.

questions.prompt: When you look at what you are chasing now, how much of it comes from genuine desire rather than proving something?

options.label: Almost all of it is about proving.
More proving than genuine desire.
About half and half.
Mostly genuine desire.
I can feel the difference and choose from what is true.

questions.prompt: In the last month, how much of your time, attention, and energy went toward what you actually want?

options.label: Very little. Most went to obligation, maintenance, or expectation.
Some, but less than I would like.
About half.
Most of it.
My time, attention, and energy are increasingly shaped by what is real for me.

questions.prompt: When you complain about the pace, the relationship, the work, or the life you have built, what usually happens next?

options.label: Nothing. The complaint repeats.
I vent, then return to the same pattern.
I sometimes see what the complaint is pointing to.
I often use it to find what I actually want or need to face.
Complaint quickly becomes information, choice, or action.

questions.prompt: When life interrupts your plan, what usually happens to your desire?

options.label: I lose contact with it and go into survival.
I become rigid and try to force the old plan.
I adjust, but it costs me a lot.
I stay connected to what matters and adapt.
I let the interruption reveal a more honest desire.

questions.prompt: After a long stretch of high output, what actually brings you back to yourself?

options.label: Nothing reliable. I just wait until I recover.
Rest helps, but I do not feel truly restored.
A few things help, but they are inconsistent.
I have practices that reliably restore me.
I know how to return to my body, energy, and clarity.

questions.prompt: You can probably name the pattern that keeps showing up under pressure. How often does knowing it actually change how you live?

options.label: Rarely. Knowing and changing feel separate.
Occasionally.
Sometimes.
Often.
Almost always. Insight moves into behaviour, body, and choice.

questions.prompt: What happens to the resentment, grief, unfinished conversations, and old experiences you still carry?

options.label: They mostly accumulate.
Some fade, but little gets consciously cleared.
Some gets worked through over time.
Most of it gets faced and moved.
I have reliable ways to clear what I carry.
